chunk_summary: This section outlines incident response and breach notification workflows for child health data in Indian tele-nutrition, emphasizing containment, notification duties, and return-to-care steps to ensure ethical and equitable practices.
learning_objectives: Understand the importance of incident response workflows in tele-nutrition services for child health data.
Learn the first-hour containment actions for cyber incidents, including device isolation and evidence preservation.
Explore notification requirements under CERT-In and DPDP for data breaches and patient communication strategies.
Develop a breach playbook with defined roles and proactive return-to-care measures.
key_facts: CERT-In mandates reporting certain cyber incidents within hours of detection.
Under DPDP, personal data breaches require notification to the Data Protection Board of India and affected individuals.
First-hour actions include disabling accounts, revoking sessions, changing passwords, and preserving logs for audits.
Clinics must contractually require vendor cooperation for breach reporting and support.
Return-to-care steps include recreating registries from backups and prioritizing high-risk children for follow-up.
Evidence-based supportive care measures are essential
reference_quote: Under DPDP, personal data breaches require notification to the Data Protection Board of India and affected individuals when applicable; plan patient-facing messages that are factual, non-alarming, and include practical steps.
document_key_topics: Incident response workflows for tele-nutrition services
Breach notification requirements under CERT-In and DPDP
Ethical and equitable practices in child health data management
